# CONVENTIONS.md

This file is the authoritative source of structured and mechanical conventions this project follows. Each block below states a rule with a stable citable ID that reviewers and the AI agent reference in commits, PR descriptions, and code reviews.

These conventions were adopted from the camerata principle library by the project's architect. The rules represent committed choices, not suggestions. Structured rules are verified by code review and citation; mechanical rules can be enforced by lint, type-system, or CI checks.

The companion file AGENTS.md holds this project's prose-enforcement directives, the behavioral guidance the agent applies by judgment. The agent reads both files when starting work.

When applying a rule, cite its ID in the commit body or PR description (for example, per RUST-DOMAIN-6).

Do not edit this file by hand. To change the rules, edit the project's principle selection and regenerate via camerata.

---

## Universal

### ARCH-FETCH-THEN-AUTHORIZE-1 — Authorization reads the fetched resource and produces an authorized-typed value
Per-resource authorization runs after the resource is fetched and before business logic runs. A dedicated authorization step reads the fetched resource, applies the policy, and on success produces a value whose type marks it as authorized. Business logic accepts only that authorized-typed value, so an unauthorized resource cannot reach it. The fetch-authorize-act order is fixed; business logic never re-checks and never receives an unchecked resource.

### ARCH-NO-SECRETS-IN-URL-1 — Secrets and credentials travel in the request body or headers, never a URL; credential-carrying forms POST so they stay safe before hydration
Credentials, tokens, secrets, and PII are transmitted only in the request body or in headers, never in a URL path or query string: no token, password, or session value as a query parameter, and no secret in a path segment. Every form that carries a credential or secret sets its method to POST explicitly, so a native submission (before the client bundle hydrates, or with JavaScript disabled or failed) posts the values in the request body rather than falling back to a GET that serializes them into the URL. Endpoints that accept secrets reject them when supplied via the query string. This is defense-in-depth: the protection must hold even when the client-side script that would intercept the submit has not run.

### ARCH-SERVER-AUTHZ-1 — Authorization on the server; UI renders permission hints
Each resource response carries server-computed permission flags (e.g. _can: { manage: true }); the UI gates affordances on those flags and never recomputes permission logic client-side. Block client-side permission introspection with lint.

_Conformance:_ Enforced by a lint rule (an ESLint no-restricted-imports / no-restricted-syntax rule or the stack equivalent) that blocks client-side permission introspection: the UI may not import the raw permission-code or role-membership helpers, only read the server-computed permission flags carried on each resource response. Conformance holds when that rule runs in CI and a client component that re-implements an authorization decision from raw codes fails the build; it fails when no rule blocks client-side introspection and the UI can recompute permissions in the browser.

### ORCH-AUTOCALLS-LEDGER-1 — Auto-calls ledger + periodic judgment review
Every self-made clear-winner call is logged with date, rule, decision, alternatives, and why. The agent surfaces the accumulated batch for human accept/modify/reject on a weekly cadence. Further autonomous runs hold until the batch is cleared, so drift cannot compound past the weekly review window.

### ORCH-CONFORMANCE-1 — Codified architectural commitments are enforced gates, not advisory documents
The architectural commitments the project codifies for its autonomous agents are enforced as gates on changes, not held as advisory documents. A deterministic check (a lint rule, a type-system constraint, an AST scan, an AI reviewer reading against the rules, or a combination of those) is wired into the change pipeline. A change that violates a commitment is rejected, escalated, or carries an explicit waiver naming the commitment, the violation, and the reviewer who waived it. Waivers are recorded in a durable log so periodic human review can sweep them and decide whether the commitment needs refinement.

_Conformance:_ Prose check: a deterministic gate (a lint rule, a type-system constraint, an AST scan, a CI-run AI reviewer reading against the codified commitments, or a combination) is wired into the change pipeline, and a durable waiver log exists. Conformance holds when the pipeline configuration names that gate and a change that violates a known commitment is demonstrably blocked, escalated, or waivered with the violation and reviewer recorded, rather than merged silently. It fails when the commitments live only as documents that reviewers cite ad hoc with no mechanical step on the agent's output.

### ORCH-ENV-GATED-QUALITY-1 — Environment-gated quality — nothing ships unverified
Autonomously-generated code passes a local quality bar (compile clean, lint clean, tests green; schema changes also pass a migration-safety check) before merge. After merge, the code progresses through one or more pre-production environments before reaching users, and advancement through each one is gated by automated checks rather than reviewer judgment. The number, naming, and topology of pre-production environments is the organization's call (single staging, dev-then-staging, ephemeral preview-per-PR, canary slices of the production environment, or any combination); the rule commits to the existence of the gradient and to automated gating between its steps, not to a specific environment layout.

_Conformance:_ Prose check: the pre-merge pipeline runs a local quality bar (a clean compile, a clean lint pass, a green test run, plus a migration-safety check on schema changes) and fails the change if any step is red. At least one pre-production environment sits between merge and users, and advancement through it is triggered by an automated check rather than a manual approval click. Conformance holds when the CI configuration shows both the pre-merge gate and an automated promotion gate with no human-approval step between merge and the user-facing environment; it fails when code can reach users straight from a developer machine or by a reviewer pressing a button.

### ORCH-NEW-PATH-TESTS-1 — AI-authored code paths carry tests, not just a passing existing suite
A change that adds a new code path carries new tests for that path, not only a green run of the existing test suite. The new test exercises the new branch directly, and a change-time check verifies that the new path is actually reached by the new test (a coverage diff on the introduced lines, a mutation check on the new branch, or equivalent). The rule applies to every change but is load-bearing for AI-authored ones, because agents can write code that compiles and that the existing suite passes while leaving the new branch entirely uncovered.

_Conformance:_ Prose check: a change-time gate verifies that the new code paths a diff introduces are actually exercised by tests in the same diff, not merely that the pre-existing suite stays green. The realistic mechanism is a per-diff coverage check on the introduced lines (a patch-coverage gate that fails when newly-added lines are uncovered) or an equivalent mutation check on the new branches. Conformance holds when the pipeline runs such a per-diff check and a diff that adds an untested branch is rejected; it fails when the only gate is a whole-project coverage percentage or a green run of the existing suite, both of which can pass while the new branch is entirely uncovered.

### ORCH-PREREVIEW-1 — Every change carries an AI reviewer's verdict into the human review queue
Code changes authored by autonomous agents pass through an AI review step. A reviewer agent reads the diff against the project's codified architectural commitments and either approves the change, requests specific changes, or escalates the change as out of scope. The human review queue contains only changes the reviewer agent has cleared or has explicitly flagged for human attention. The reviewer step is wired into the change pipeline (a pre-merge hook, a CI job, a bot on the pull request, or equivalent) such that the change cannot reach the human merge gate without an approval status from the reviewer agent.

_Conformance:_ Prose check (applies once the project adopts the AI-pre-review position): an AI reviewer step is wired into the change pipeline as a pre-merge hook, a CI job, or a bot on the pull request, such that a change cannot reach the human merge gate without an approval status from the reviewer agent. Conformance holds when the pipeline configuration shows that required reviewer status and an unreviewed change is blocked from the human queue; it fails when AI-authored changes flow to human review with no enforced reviewer-agent verdict gating them.

### ORCH-REVIEWER-SPLIT-1 — The reviewer agent for any change is distinct from the author agent
The agent that reviews a change is a separate agent from the one that authored it. Author and reviewer differ in some load-bearing configuration: a different prompt, a different principles set, a different model, or a combination of those. The reviewer reads the diff, the change's stated intent, and the codified architectural commitments. The reviewer does not read the author agent's reasoning trace. This separation holds across all orchestrated work regardless of whether author and reviewer share a model family.

### PROC-CI-MIGRATION-HYGIENE-1 — CI hygiene — enforce migration idempotency + safety at the gate
For SQL systems: lint every migration in CI. Require idempotency (IF NOT EXISTS), require explicit opt-in for destructive ops, and guard against migration-journal index collisions, all before the build.

_Conformance:_ Enforced by a CI migration-lint step that scans every migration in the change before the build: it requires idempotency guards (IF NOT EXISTS on creates), requires an explicit opt-in marker for destructive operations (DROP, TRUNCATE, column removal), and checks for migration-journal index/sequence-number collisions. Conformance holds when that lint job runs on every PR and a migration missing an IF NOT EXISTS guard, containing an unmarked destructive op, or colliding on the journal index fails the gate; it fails when migrations rely on human review alone with no mechanical CI check.

### PROC-CITE-CONVENTION-ID-1 — Cite the convention ID in commit messages and PR descriptions
A commit (or PR description) that applies a documented convention cites its ID in the body, for example 'per ENTITIES-4' or 'follows RUST-DOMAIN-2'. The citation lives in the commit body, not the subject line, so the convention reference does not crowd the headline.

### PROC-HIDE-DEAD-END-1 — Hide dead-end affordances
For permission-gated UIs: never render a UI action the user's permissions can't execute. Gate each affordance's render on the same permission its endpoint requires. A button that 403s drives the user to an error page; that redirect IS the bug.

### PROC-PR-CONCURRENCY-1 — PR template forces a concurrency story
PRs touching shared mutable state must document the locking strategy and concurrency testing in a required template section. Skippable for read-only / UI / docs PRs.

### PROC-REGRESSION-TEST-1 — Every bug fix ships with a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after
A change that fixes a bug includes, in the same change, a test that fails on the pre-fix code and passes on the post-fix code. The test lives at whichever layer of the test pyramid naturally exercises the bug (unit if the bug is in pure logic, integration if it is in component wiring, end-to-end if it is in a user-visible flow). The author confirms the failing-then-passing behavior before submitting the change. A bug-fix change with no accompanying test, or with a test that passes on the pre-fix code as well, is incomplete regardless of whether the surrounding suite goes green.

### SPIRIT-DOC-DECISIONS-1 — Document decisions immediately
Every decision (architectural, process, convention, even small) is written to a persistent artifact the moment it is made: a conventions file with a rule ID for code, a dated decision doc for process.

### SQL-AUDIT-COLUMNS-1 — Three audit-column strategies; pick by table semantics
Pick one of three audit-column strategies per table, by what produces the rows.

Admin-curated reference tables (categories, roles, system config, lookup tables): created_by UUID + created_on timestamp + modified_by UUID + modified_on timestamp. Multiple admins edit these rows and modified_by is consumed by review tooling.

User-generated content tables (posts, comments, applications, submissions): created_at timestamp + updated_at timestamp + an author foreign key. The author FK already attributes the row; separate created_by and modified_by would duplicate it.

System-managed event or log tables (audit_log, ingest_events, webhook_deliveries): domain-specific timestamps (received_at, processed_at, occurred_at) and no created_by or modified_by. The row's origin is the system, not a user.

### SQL-DB-INDEX-1 — Every foreign-key column gets an index in the same migration
When a migration adds a foreign-key column, the same migration adds an index on it. Declare the index adjacent to the FK in the file so a reviewer can see them together. The pair is enforceable by a migration linter that scans for FK declarations and verifies a matching index in the same change.

_Conformance:_ Enforced by a migration linter that scans each migration for foreign-key declarations (REFERENCES clauses or the ORM's FK helper) and verifies a matching index on the same column lands in the same change. The linter can also run as a one-shot audit query against the live schema that lists every FK-constrained column with no covering index and returns rows for any gap. Conformance holds when the per-migration check runs in CI and the audit query returns zero rows; it fails when an FK column ships with no index in the same migration.

### SQL-DB-INDEX-2 — Every WHERE-clause column has a supporting index
Columns used in WHERE predicates (and in ORDER BY/JOIN keys) have a supporting index. For composite filters, the most-selective column comes first in the index.

_Conformance:_ Prose check, enforced by query-plan inspection in CI: representative queries run under EXPLAIN (or the engine's plan tool) and the gate fails on a sequential/full-table scan over a filtered, ordered, or joined column that should be index-backed, with composite indexes ordered most-selective-column-first. The realistic mechanism is a slow-query / missing-index audit (pg_stat_statements plus an unused-or-missing-index report, or the engine equivalent) run on a representative dataset. Conformance holds when that audit runs and surfaces no unindexed WHERE/ORDER BY/JOIN column on hot paths; it fails when indexes are only added reactively after production queries slow down.

### SQL-DB-NPLUSONE-1 — No N+1 query patterns
Iterating a collection with a per-item database call is banned. Use a single batched query (or 1-2 queries with a join / eager-load), never N+1.

_Conformance:_ Prose check: a query-count assertion in tests exercises each list-returning code path and fails when the number of database round-trips grows with the size of the result set rather than staying constant (the textbook N+1 signature). The realistic mechanism is a test-harness query counter (an ORM query-log assertion, a per-request query-count budget, or a strict-loading mode that raises on lazy N+1 access) plus review for loops issuing a per-item query. Conformance holds when such a query-count guard runs in CI and a loop-with-per-item-query path fails it; it fails when no per-path query-count check exists and N+1 patterns can merge unnoticed.

## Rust

### RUST-DOMAIN-1 — Single domain crate, modules by bounded context
One domain crate. Bounded contexts are internal modules, not separate crates.

### RUST-DOMAIN-2 — Newtype IDs (every ID is a wrapper, never a bare Uuid)
UserId(Uuid), OrganizationId(Uuid); bare Uuid only at persistence and HTTP boundaries.

### RUST-DOMAIN-3 — Newtype validated strings (Email, Url, Slug, …)
Constrained string types are newtypes with a fallible constructor (Email::try_new); the type itself is proof of validation. Free-form text stays as String.

### RUST-DOMAIN-4 — Errors via thiserror enums per crate
Each layer's crate defines its own thiserror enum (DomainError, RepositoryError, ServiceError, …) with explicit From impls at boundaries. anyhow is reserved for application edges (binaries).

### RUST-DOMAIN-6 — Errors are category-scoped, one shared enum per failure category at the crate boundary
Each Rust crate exposes errors organized by failure category at its public boundary, with one shared enum per category. The categories are whatever distinct failure surfaces the crate exposes; examples include persistence and IO failures, input validation, business rule violations, parsing, and networking, but the actual set depends on what the crate does. Variants within an enum enumerate the distinct failure modes for that category. Driver-specific or framework-specific errors map into the appropriate category enum at the boundary; they do not leak through the public API directly. Per-function or per-aggregate error types are not used.

### RUST-DOMAIN-7 — Explicit UnitOfWork parameter on transactional repository methods
Repository write methods that may participate in a multi-write transaction take an explicit Option of UnitOfWork (a marker trait defined in the domain crate so the trait surface carries no concrete ORM or database type). Infrastructure implements UnitOfWork on its concrete transaction wrapper and downcasts internally via Any to access the underlying ORM transaction. Services wrap multi-write operations in a closure run on the unit of work and pass Some(uow) to each participating repo call.

## UI architecture (logic, state, and the renderer boundary)

### RUST-HEADLESS-CORE-1 — UI logic and state live in a framework-agnostic core; the rendering framework is a thin adapter (applications, not just libraries)
A UI's logic, state, and computation live in a framework-agnostic core that carries NO dependency on any rendering framework, with the rendering framework confined to a thin adapter that wires the core's state and behavior to that framework's primitives. This applies to BOTH a reusable component/UI library AND a first-party application UI: the core concept, keeping the renderer out of the logic/state layer so it is unit-testable in isolation (no virtual DOM, no component runtime) and reusable across adapters, is the same regardless of whether the code is shipped to others. The enforceable form is a separate core crate, which the compiler then guarantees stays renderer-free: a reusable library MUST use separate core plus per-framework adapter crates, and an application SHOULD too (a strict, dependency-enforced internal module boundary is the weaker minimum, since nothing stops a plain module from importing the renderer). Framework-specific code lives only in the adapter. This rule governs STRUCTURE (logic/state separated from the renderer); its complement RUST-PURE-STATE-TRANSITIONS-1 governs the FORM of the core's state changes. The two are a matched pair, not duplicates.

### RUST-PURE-STATE-TRANSITIONS-1 — Core state changes are pure functions from old state to new state, with side effects pushed to the edges
Core state changes are expressed as pure functions that take the current state and an input and return the next state, with no input-output, no scheduling, and no mutation of anything outside the returned value. Side effects (rendering, persistence, network, logging) are performed by the caller at the edges after applying the returned state, never inside the transition. The core's transition functions are deterministic and testable in isolation with no environment. This is the complement of RUST-HEADLESS-CORE-1: that rule puts logic/state in a renderer-free core (structure); this rule constrains the FORM of the transitions within it. Satisfying one does not imply the other.
